seoTitle: How to Use Typeform Logic for Conditional Branching
metaDescription: Learn how Typeform Logic lets you show different questions based on answers, skip questions, and improve form completion rates.
intro: A form that asks the same questions to everyone can feel long and irrelevant to some respondents. Typeform Logic solves this by adapting the form based on how someone answers. This guide covers how to set up conditional branching so your surveys, quizzes, and lead forms feel more personalized.

steps.heading: Open the Logic Builder
steps.body: Access the Logic tab in your Typeform editor to start defining rules that control the order and visibility of questions.

steps.heading: Create conditional branches
steps.body: Set rules that send respondents to different questions depending on how they answer, so each path stays relevant to them.

steps.heading: Add logic rules
steps.body: Build out specific conditions, such as skipping a section entirely if a particular answer is chosen, to streamline the respondent's path.

steps.heading: Test and publish your form
steps.body: Walk through the form yourself using different answer combinations to confirm the logic behaves as expected before publishing.

faq.question: What does Typeform Logic actually do?
faq.answer: It lets you show, skip, or reorder questions based on a respondent's previous answers, creating a personalized path through the form.

faq.question: Can logic improve completion rates?
faq.answer: Yes, by skipping irrelevant questions, logic keeps forms shorter and more relevant for each respondent, which can help improve completion rates.

faq.question: Is Typeform Logic available on quizzes as well as surveys?
faq.answer: Yes, logic can be used across surveys, quizzes, lead generation forms, and customer feedback forms.

faq.question: How do I test that my logic rules work correctly?
faq.answer: Preview the form and go through it multiple times with different answers to confirm each path leads where you expect before publishing.
